title: Fix module tagger metadata serialization — update_task expects string, gets dict
analysis:
The write-side serialization bug (dict passed where string expected) is ALREADY FIXED in scheduler.update_task() at line 229-233 — it accepts `str | dict` and json.dumps dicts before the MCP call. The fused-memory server's update_task tool (tools.py:700-732) also independently coerces dicts, and has tests for it (test_health_endpoint.py:60-85). Callers: harness._tag_task_modules already pre-serializes (line 381), harness._tag_prd_metadata passes a dict (line 259) which the scheduler handles, handle_blast_radius_expansion pre-serializes (line 375) with a redundant `import json` (line 374, json is already imported at line 5).

The REAL remaining bug is on the READ SIDE: _get_modules() (line 390-413) only handles dict metadata (`isinstance(metadata, dict)`). Taskmaster stores metadata as a string internally — the taskmaster_client.update_task signature is `metadata: str | None` (line 211). When get_tasks() returns tasks, metadata may come back as a JSON string like '{"modules": ["backend"]}'. In that case, _get_modules falls through to the `task-<id>` fallback silently, defeating the entire concurrency locking system.

Additionally, the fallback to `task-<id>` is completely silent — no log output indicates when module extraction fails and generic locks are used. This makes the degradation invisible in production logs.

Plan: (1) Add tests + fix for _get_modules to parse JSON string metadata, (2) Add warning log for fallback, (3) Add regression tests for the write-side serialization, (4) Clean up redundant import.

--- POST-REVIEW ADDENDUM ---
Review identified log_flooding issue: the fallback WARNING in _get_modules fires on every poll cycle for every untagged task (thousands of times per run). Fix: add a `self._fallback_warned: set[str]` on the Scheduler instance and gate the warning to fire only once per task ID.

steps.description: Write failing test `test_get_modules_extracts_modules_from_json_string_metadata`: create a Scheduler, call _get_modules with a task dict where metadata is a JSON string '{"modules": ["backend", "server"]}'. Assert it returns the normalized module list, NOT the task-<id> fallback. This will FAIL because _get_modules only handles dict metadata (isinstance check on line 401).

Write failing test `test_get_modules_extracts_files_from_json_string_metadata`: create a Scheduler, call _get_modules with a task dict where metadata is a JSON string '{"files": ["src/server/app.py", "src/server/routes.py"]}'. Assert it returns files_to_modules-derived modules, NOT the fallback. This will also FAIL for the same reason.

Fix _get_modules() in scheduler.py: before the `isinstance(metadata, dict)` check, add a block that parses JSON string metadata: `if isinstance(metadata, str): try: metadata = json.loads(metadata) except (json.JSONDecodeError, TypeError): pass`. This allows both dict and string metadata to flow into the existing files/modules extraction logic. Both step-1 and step-2 tests should now pass.

Write test `test_get_modules_handles_malformed_json_string_metadata`: call _get_modules with metadata='not valid json'. Assert it returns the task-<id> fallback gracefully (no exception raised). This should PASS with the try/except from step-3, but confirms graceful degradation.

Write failing test `test_get_modules_logs_warning_on_fallback`: call _get_modules with a task that has no metadata (empty dict). Use caplog to assert a WARNING-level log is emitted mentioning the task ID and 'fallback'. This will FAIL because the current fallback path at line 413 has no logging.

Add a warning log to the fallback path in _get_modules() (line 413): `logger.warning('Task %s: no module metadata found — using fallback lock task-%s', task_id, task_id)` before the return statement. Step-5 test should now pass.

Write regression test `test_update_task_serializes_dict_to_json_string`: monkeypatch mcp_call, call scheduler.update_task('1', {'modules': ['backend']}), assert the MCP call's arguments dict has metadata as a JSON string (not a dict). This should PASS immediately — it's a regression guard for the existing fix at line 232.

Write regression test `test_update_task_passes_string_metadata_through`: monkeypatch mcp_call, call scheduler.update_task('1', '{"modules": ["backend"]}'), assert the MCP call's arguments dict has the same string. Verifies string metadata is not double-serialized.

Clean up redundant `import json` at line 374 inside handle_blast_radius_expansion() — json is already imported at file top (line 5). Also simplify the call on line 375 from `json.dumps({'modules': needed})` to just `{'modules': needed}` since update_task handles dict serialization. All existing tests should remain green.

Write failing test `test_get_modules_fallback_warning_emitted_only_once`: create a Scheduler, call _get_modules TWICE with the same task (id='9', metadata={}). Use caplog to assert the WARNING mentioning task 9 appears exactly ONCE in the log records (not twice). This will FAIL because the current implementation logs unconditionally on every call — there is no dedup guard.

Add `self._fallback_warned: set[str] = set()` to Scheduler.__init__ (after _status_cache). In _get_modules(), gate the logger.warning call: only emit if `task_id not in self._fallback_warned`, then add `self._fallback_warned.add(task_id)` after logging. This ensures the fallback warning fires at most once per task ID per Scheduler lifetime. Step-10 test should now pass, and step-5's existing test (single call) remains green.

design_decisions.decision: Fix the read side (_get_modules) rather than only the write side (update_task)
design_decisions.rationale: The write-side fix (scheduler.update_task dict→JSON coercion) is already in place. But _get_modules only handles dict metadata. Taskmaster stores metadata as a string internally (taskmaster_client.update_task has `metadata: str | None`), so when tasks are read back, metadata may be a JSON string. Fixing only the write side leaves the read side broken — modules are persisted correctly but can't be extracted, so locks still fall back to task-<id>.

design_decisions.decision: Parse string metadata in _get_modules rather than in get_tasks
design_decisions.rationale: _get_modules is the single consumer of metadata.files and metadata.modules. Parsing at the consumption point is more robust (handles both dict and string regardless of transport) and doesn't risk side effects in the general-purpose get_tasks method. Keeps the fix localized.

design_decisions.decision: Keep task-<id> fallback as intentional safe degradation, but add WARNING log
design_decisions.rationale: The task-<id> fallback is safe — it over-serializes (no two tasks run concurrently on the same generic lock) rather than under-serializing. This is correct defensive behavior. However, silent fallback made the bug invisible in production. A WARNING log makes the degradation visible without changing the safety behavior.

design_decisions.decision: Deduplicate fallback warning with per-instance _fallback_warned set
design_decisions.rationale: _get_modules() is called on every scheduler poll cycle for every pending task. Without dedup, tasks that legitimately lack module metadata (pre-convention tasks, failed update_task calls) emit the warning thousands of times per run, drowning out genuine warnings. A set[str] on the Scheduler instance tracks which task IDs have been warned; the warning fires once per task ID per Scheduler lifetime. This preserves the diagnostic value (first occurrence is logged) while eliminating log flooding.

design_decisions.decision: Do not change harness._tag_prd_metadata caller to pre-serialize
design_decisions.rationale: The harness passes a dict {'prd': resolved_prd} to scheduler.update_task, which handles serialization. Changing the harness for 'consistency' would require updating test_harness_prd_tagging.py assertions and adds no safety value since both scheduler.update_task AND the fused-memory server handle dict coercion. The existing defense-in-depth (double coercion at scheduler + server) is sufficient.

design_decisions.decision: Simplify handle_blast_radius_expansion to pass dict instead of pre-serialized JSON string
design_decisions.rationale: Removes the redundant `import json` (line 374) and makes the calling convention consistent: all callers pass dicts, scheduler.update_task handles serialization. The pre-serialization was a workaround for the original bug; with the scheduler fix in place, it's unnecessary indirection.

reuse.how: Reuse the same pattern: create OrchestratorConfig(max_per_module=1), instantiate Scheduler(config). Used in all scheduler test classes.
monkeypatch.setattr('orchestrator.scheduler.mcp_call', AsyncMock(return_value={})) — intercepts MCP calls to verify arguments without network.
Use caplog.at_level(logging.WARNING, logger='orchestrator.scheduler') to capture and assert warning log messages in the fallback path.
The existing `isinstance(metadata, dict) → json.dumps` pattern is the template for the analogous fix in _get_modules (string → json.loads).

reuse.what: fused-memory server-side dict coercion pattern and tests
reuse.how: The server already handles dict metadata coercion with tests. This is defense-in-depth — both client (scheduler) and server handle the conversion.
